reasoning: The mathematical trajectory of renewable capacity growth virtually guarantees solar will eclipse hydro and wind by 2030, absent severe regulatory or geopolitical shocks (e.g., a trade war collapsing Chinese solar panel exports). Based on the Energy Institute's 2024 consumption data, Hydro is the top renewable at ~16 EJ, wind is second at 9.04 EJ, and solar is third at 7.72 EJ. However, solar generation is growing at a compounding 25-30% annually, while wind grows softly (~10-12%) and hydro is essentially stationary (~1-2%). Forecasting these out, solar generation will exceed wind around 2026 and hydro around 2028. Notably, in 2024 the Energy Institute abandoned the substitution method (which previously applied the same ~2.6x multiplier to renewable primary energy based on fossil fuel equivalents), meaning the metric is now tied directly to pure generation in exajoules. The IEA's usually conservative "Renewables 2024" report also officially projects solar to pass hydropower in 2029. Given the seven-year runway to 2030, the probability is exceptionally high.
